summary: Cantrill introduces three new DTrace aggregation display options developed from Brendan Gregg's practical observations: agghist for histogram-style visualization of count/sum aggregations, aggzoom for scaling bar heights to the maximum value rather than distributing across all bars, and aggpack for compact single-line histogram representation using Unicode block elements. These features exemplify DTrace's evolution as a 'workhorse' tool where improvements come from real production needs rather than theoretical concerns.
key_points: New agghist option adds histogram-style bars to count()/sum() aggregation output for easier visual differentiation
aggzoom fixes the long-standing whitespace problem by scaling bars relative to the maximum value rather than distributing height across all bars
aggpack provides dramatic output compression by rotating histograms 90 degrees, fitting each key's distribution on a single line
Unicode Block Elements enable more granular visualization than ASCII characters in terminal output
All features originated from Brendan Gregg's practical observations while using DTrace in production
money_quotes: DTrace is a workhorse, not a show horse: the features that we have added to DTrace over the years come not from theoretical notions but rather from actual needs in production environments.

This communicates essentially the same amount of information in just a fraction of the output (55 lines versus 482) – and makes it (much) easier to quickly compare distributions across aggregation keys.
DTrace only improves when those of us who depend on it every day envision a way that it could be even better!

Due to some combination of my laziness and my reverence for the lockstat-style histogram, I simply lifted the lockstat processing code – which means I inherited (or stole, depending on your perspective) the decisions Jeff had made with regard to histogram rendering.
key_insight: The best developer tools evolve through the accumulated practical insights of power users who encounter real limitations in production environments, not through theoretical feature planning.
